chunk_summary: Guidelines for diagnosing and managing thinness in children aged 5-19 years using BMI-for-age classification, with emphasis on differential diagnosis, clinical triage, and program linkages to improve nutritional outcomes.
learning_objectives: Understand the BMI-for-age classification for diagnosing thinness and severe thinness in children aged 5-19 years.
Identify clinical and psychosocial factors contributing to thinness in this age group.
Learn management strategies, including dietary plans and program linkages, for addressing moderate acute malnutrition.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and monitoring BMI trends over time.
key_facts: Thinness is defined as BMI-for-age (BAZ) below -2, and severe thinness as BAZ below -3 (WHO growth reference).
Always interpret BMI-for-age alongside height-for-age to account for stunting effects.
Urgent escalation is required for danger signs like rapid weight loss, dehydration, or suspected severe illness.
Management includes three meals, two nutritious snacks, daily protein, and fat add-ons.
Linkages to government programs like PM POSHAN (school meals), RKSK (adolescent health), and WIFS (anaemia prevention) are critical.
Follow-up should document weight and BMI trends rather than relying on single measurements.
reference_quote: Thinness is body mass index-for-age (BAZ) below -2, and severe thinness below -3. Always interpret BAZ together with height-for-age because a short child may appear 'less thin' by BMI even with significant undernutrition.
document_key_topics: Differential diagnosis of thinness in ages 5-19
